item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I live and grew up on Arran and was pleasantly surprised there was an scp for the island!

Overall I think the concept of the scp is solid but there are some things that don't work for me. First one is a minor detail but the island is always referred to as the "Isle of Arran" instead of "Arran Island". This is to prevent confusion with mixing it up with the Aran Islands (spelt with one R instead of 2) in Ireland. I'd recommend changing this.

The next is one that I'd have to agree with other peoples' feedback on. Making the royalty be connected to King Arthur for a scottish cave doesn't sit right with me. I kind of get the impression that the author googled King's Cave UK to find a place that would work for the narrative. Perhaps if this was mentioned as an odd location for an arthurian site in some way in the article it would be less of an issue for people. Or you could say that the cave had some connection to merlin as there is a theory that he was a druid in the lowlands of scotland. Or another idea, Arran was previously owned by norse vikings hundreds of years ago, could maybe rewrite it to work with some norse mythology?

Another thing you could add as it's a real location. A fence and gate was installed on the actual cave in the 90s to prevent people damaging the cave and the cave drawings that are inside it, could easily add this into the story as a way for the scp to stop incedents occuring?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Approved!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Man, when you consider this article, SCP-1337, and SCP-973, the Foundation keeps breaking the ghosts its trying to contain. In SCP-1337, the wikiwalk crosslink mentioned a internal research paper called "Apparition Escalation: Preliminary Research into Human Triggered Escalation of Apparition Hostility" by Tamlin et al. Since it mentions within the article that the Foundation is trying to keep royal descendants away from the cave to prevent potential disaster, a mention of this in-universe document (which would serve as an inline link to 1337 and 973) wouldn't be too out of line. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

SCP-1777-1 appears to be triggered by the presence of any direct descendant of any of the kings of England. This does not extend to descendants of queens. When such a person enters SCP-1777-2, SCP-1777-1 appears, and seems to study them for a moment. It will then speak in what has been identified as a dialect of Latin, 'Puer de (insert ancestor king's name) est non tempus'1 before vanishing. It is unsure why this is done, but whenever possible, known descendants of kings are kept away from SCP-1777-2.

To this:

SCP-1777-1 appears to be triggered by the presence of any direct descendant of any of the kings of England. This does not extend to descendants of queens. When such a person enters SCP-1777-2, SCP-1777-1 appears, and seems to study them for a moment. It will then speak in what has been identified as a dialect of Latin, 'Puer de (insert ancestor king's name) est non tempus'2 before vanishing. It is unsure why this is done, but whenever possible, known descendants of kings are kept away from SCP-1777-23.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Child of ( ) it is not your time.
2. Child of ( ) it is not your time.
3. Per Tamlin Apparition Protocols. See "Apparition Escalation: Preliminary Research into Human Triggered Escalation of Apparition Hostility" by Tamlin et al, for additional examples.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The clinical tone here… needs work, I think. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mainly due to two reasons. One: Arran Island, and Kings Cave, is an ACTUAL place, said to be the final resting place of Arthur. Two: Avalon was across the sea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just not-bad SCP article, but why Scotland? The background of matter of Britain is Wales, not Scots…
Well this may be just a nitpick, but it make me nervous that discordance between mythological thingys and their original source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Not exceedingly, but enough. For some reason, it doesn't completely click with me, no idea why. The main issue I have, anyway, is about the Latin sentences, Google Translate really doesn't know how to translate Latin. I ask permission to correct them, because I'm a grammarnazi!
Anyway, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the sword disappear from Foundation custody and materialize wedged in a rock somewhere at some point?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are no ravens there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh fuck. Albion is doomed now. Someone check the Tower of London.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OMG this comment is beautiful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The stated fact that carvings on the pillar are reflected on the armor of the knight makes me picture him with little carved hearts, "JLP + SCD 4-EVA", and random curse words all over him…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… the story this one is telling is too blatant for me. It has all the ingredients I like in an SCP that explores personnel in the Foundation and has undercurrents about human nature and stuff, but it's executed a little bit too blatantly for me to enjoy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YOU MANIACS! YOU KILLED KING ARTHUR! OH, DAMN YOU! GODDAMN YOU ALL TO HELL!!!

(On the other hands, a man with heart defect probably won't be able to sustain a boardsword-swingin', plate-armour-wearin', going-around-to-conquer-all-thing lifestyle for long anyway)

Personally, I'd like to see the article fleshed out a bit more (maybe with a couple more nods to Arthurian legends?), but it's pretty good as it is. Upvoted.

Now to the Big Question: did the Foundation remember to collect Mr. Scrivens' DNA/preserve his remains after his death? And has there been at least a proposal for a future "cloning King Arthur" project?

item.title: Re: Slightly off-topic, but...
item.content:encoded: Thank you for saying succinctly what I have been thinking for weeks. You are my hero, Moose. SCP's have a particular format, and should strive for a professional and scientific feel. They do not need to read like they were written by robots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Themes remind me a bit of the most recent arc of Hellboy, in a good way. The only thing that I stuck on was "Sweyn" because hey that sounds Norse.

—Because it is Norse, apparently. Nice historical tidbit.

Any SCP that pulls off elements like swords and history/myth with style is an accomplishment. I enjoyed this one quite a bit, and the readable tone was contributory.

Edit: Now I'll spend the rest of my evening staring at "Stultus bestia" and wondering if it is in the vocative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that was depressing. And therefore perfectly fitting for the SCP 'verse.

item.title: Slightly off-topic, but...
item.content:encoded:
I really don't agree with the tone things. scientists are not another breed of people, they still speak english.

I do agree that a few of those phrases could stand to be changed, but most of them are just fine.

I've noticed a lot of feedback like this lately. "Bad tone" gets thrown around a lot in places where it doesn't really apply, usually (though not always) by newer members.

Now, it's true that in the real world, many academics nitpick writing in scientific articles to death, and criticize articles harshly for any hint of interesting prose.

Other academics think this is total bullshit. I happen to agree with them.

Please note in the Foundation, no one has to worry about their article not getting published in a peer-reviewed magazine because they didn't conform to a somewhat silly standard they didn't agree with.

In other words: Being melodramatic is still to be avoided, but using words like "backhanding" and "clasped" is perfectly fine. And I wish people would stop saying otherwise, because from where I'm standing that's shitty feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was kind of going on the 'hidden' side of things. Arthur was taken away to a mythical isle…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. I suppose we'll have to agree to disagree on the tone here.

I wasn't saying that you had to use one definitively linked to the Arthurian legend, since I agree that would be really obvious. But the choice to set an SCP based so heavily on English and Welsh lore in Scotland is still odd. This would mean that the entity who was supposed to deliver Excalibur to Arthur's chosen successor decided to set up shop in what was, until 1707, a completely different country. It's like finding a mythical German artefact in an ancient temple in northern Italy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't agree with the tone things. scientists are not another breed of people, they still speak english.

and I chose Kings Cave BECAUSE using King Arthurs cave or Merlins cave would be too much of a hammer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry that I had to downvote this one. The idea here is brilliant, and I really like it as a tale of subverted destiny. I don't, however, like it as an SCP article. There's just too many minor issues for me to upvote in good conscious.

Researchers are encouraged not to attempt interaction with SCP-1777-2.

Unless the researchers aren't allowed to interact with a cave, I think you mean SCP-1777-1.

…SCP-1777-1 is an apparent specter…

Firstly, I don't think 'specter' fits the site's tone very well. It's a term too close to folklore and ghost stories to appear in an official document. That, and the use of 'apparent' here is simply clunky. 'Entity' would probably be a better fit.

Clasped in its hands is a cross hilted longsword approximately 1.5 meter long, bearing a double fuller style blade.

'Clasped' doesn't sound right in this context. It feels like the first half of the sentence is from a narrative, and the second is from a report.

Testing has proven anything carved on this column will show up on the armor of SCP-1777-1 when it appears.

'Will show up' is, again, not the sort of thing I expect you'd see in this sort of report.

It is unsure why this is done, but whenever possible, known descendants of kings are kept away from SCP-1777-2.

This whole sentence is extremely clunky. 'It is unsure why this is done' doesn't scan very well and doesn't seem scientific. Moreover, the first and last halves of the sentence don't connect very well, and the fact that everything after the 'but' should be in the containment procedures doesn't help matters.

…causing SCP-1777-1 to stay manifested for longer then it ever had before.

Again, 'than it ever had before' isn't very formal or scientific.

…backhanding the researcher, breaking his jaw.

And 'backhanding' certainly isn't either.

There are also some other things which I find problematic about this that aren't to do with the tone. The biggest is the choice of King's Cave, which I simply can't understand. Why choose to set something which is clearly based on the King Arthur legend in a cave in Scotland, named after a totally unrelated Scottish King, when the King Arthur legend is much more strongly connected to England or Wales? Also, how does the ghost respond to descendants of kings from before the unification of England?

I understand that this probably sounds like I'm nitpicking, but these slight flaws in the tone of the article added up to ruin my enjoyment of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's neutralized, far as they're concerned. Looking for others now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That house full of heart-harvesting shadow monsters? I didn't think that was considered fully neutralized yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apologies to His Excellency the Honourable Sir Professor Bright, but to me the tone seems a bit… too formal. Phrases like "so as to" and "rarity/safe nature" make it seem like it was written by someone trying desperately hard to seem formal, rather than by an actual formal person.
And I didn't like the concept either. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what, I wasn't expecting to like it, but I do.

I have one nitpick, though. The line

It is unsure why this is done, but whenever possible, known descendants of kings are kept away from SCP-1777-2.

belongs in the containment procedures, doesn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple more revised translations for ya:

2: Fili [], non est tempus tui.
3. Stultus bestia, relinquo me muneri mei.1

Also, after doing those I managed to recall a bit more of my Latin grammar knowledge and realized the first translation I gave you is actually sliiightly incorrect. If he's talking to the dead guy, 'Arturus' should, I think, properly be 'Arturi'.

Footnotes
1. From the dictionary I'm using: "munus, muneris: service; duty, office, function". You might want to change the English translation in the article to reflect that, although I think I'm just being pedantic at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was google translate, and, i fixed it. I coulda sworn i'd heard something close to what you posted before, but i trusted the internets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not promising this is perfectly accurate (I haven't studied Latin since 2008), but a better translation of the fourth phrase would be "Requiesce in pace Arturius/Arturus, sic finit Camelot."

Out of curiosity, what translator gave you 'Benedicamus domino'? The meaning of that phrase er… isn't really close to 'rest in peace'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's cool. Favorite thing about it is the fact that it's magic, no science or explanation. Also I'd get a picture of the sword, maybe elaborate on it more.

Also added that commonly forgotten "neutralized" tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was a Neutralized SCP that a D-Class stopped, number escapes me though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you can fix the latin, let me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Show me what you got kid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is something missing that didn't make me run to vote. I think I just want the idea more fleshed out, it's a little too short to really grip me. Personal preference yadda yadda.

But it wasn't so much that it kept me from +1. Love the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other than the obviously Google-translated Latin, I really dig this. It's right on the border of "poor idea", but the execution makes it work, also the fact that it's neutralized now. A story like this hasn't been done in an article, to my knowledge, and so this stands out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about a conventional, non-photoshopped multi-exposure?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Mostly because the image of a ghost backhanding Doctor James is too good to resist.

Edit: Doctor Janus. Derp. Doctor James is still the head researcher of all ghost based SCPs in my heart, though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Latin is bugging me (in particular, that last one), as is the central concept, which was the reason I downvoted the first incarnation of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The comment (not essay) in question was intended for the information of people who are capable of following up a list of references all by themselves.

You should feel free to ignore it.

Edit: and for those who don't need their forum comments to use APA style citations, Eve is our most recent common matrilineal ancestor as determined by mitochondrial DNA. The source here is "my memory," but i'm confident it will check out. Anyway it should be intuitive that our most recent common ancestor isn't female. Sorry if my last two comments are a bit on the snide side, I was struggling to tone it down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, this is perfect writing. And the Arthur twist is very neat as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is why i upvoted.

People stop being so nitpicky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it seems the odds of someone with a direct patrilineal link to the throne of England stumbling in to this cave is kind of low.

Not if he didn't stumble into the cave. He was directed there by fate, and received the sword that this SCP had been saving for him all these years so that he could fulfill his destiny.

And then the Foundation fucked everything up by killing him (or as I prefer to think of it, the Foundation saved the day because screw destiny)

Either way, the magic came to an end with his death, and the SCP was neutralized. That was not necessarily what the Foundation intended to do, but the theme of laying magic low through attention to detail and scientific process very much fits what the Foundation stands for. So ends Camelot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Come on, man, that's like using the Wikipedia source on Charlie Sheen to make the point that he's half man, half cocaine. If your college teacher wouldn't accept it as a valid source in an essay, we sure as hell won't accept it here.

Edit: And, according to this Smithsonian article, our last common ancestor was a woman named "Eve" dating from about 200,000 years ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clearly I'm not firing on all cylinders today. I meant QEII, not the Queen Mum. My bad!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I make no pretense of knowing Latin, but I think the computer might need some help, particularly with that last one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though we'll all still have to wait a couple years to find out anyway, seeing as the Queen Mum refuses to die.

She died about 10 years ago, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the first link that came up when I tried to think of the right phrase to Google from my phone, in order to direct interested people to further info.

It certainly has enough references to other sources for an intelligent person to be able to investigate further. Do you think the nature of the link somehow invalidates the point I made? If so, I am going to have to politely disregard you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd be interested to know how this was originally discovered. Unless Roving Researcher is right, and everyone is descended from everyone else, it seems the odds of someone with a direct patrilineal link to the throne of England stumbling in to this cave is kind of low.

Given what he says to descendants of kings, I'd also be interested to know what he'd say to a reigning monarch. Though we'll all still have to wait a couple years to find out anyway, seeing as the Queen Mum refuses to die.

Edit: wasn't aware that King's Cave was a destination spot! Which it kind of is. Well, that raises the likelihood of visitation. I have learned today. Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://humphrysfamilytree.com/ca.html

that's an, um, interesting source you've got there, but it's not exactly… yeah. don't reference that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But if Arthur had descendants, literally everyone in Britain would be descended from him. It is unquestionable that everyone in Britain is a descendant of a king.
http://humphrysfamilytree.com/ca.html

If you mean descended in a direct male line, then it makes more mathematical sense but the storytelling logic is iffy. (descended in a direct male line via illegitimacy shouldn't have any special significance.) I think you have to have a rightful heir who was unjustly disinherited somewhere along the line to make the storytelling logic hang together and that is obviously super cheesy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
imperial measurements are radical

fight the power

I mean come on, this is all about the kings of bloody England here. I liked this for what it was, a story about thwarted destiny. Camelot is dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed some of those issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with you all the way here. Although there seems to be some degree of hinting that the sword may, in fact, be Excalibur, possibly the Ur Example of "sword what makes you fightan good". But in that context the man would have eventually slaughtered all the guards and made his way on to claim the throne of all the Britons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dis like photoshopped pictures, they always look fake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You just summed up why I downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a six foot male human,

Non-metric usage.

a cross hilted longsword approximately 4 feet long,

Non-metric usage.

SCP-1777-1 appears to be triggered by the presence of any direct descendant of any of the kings of England.

Nitpick here. Do you literally mean "Kings of England", i.e. male sovereigns, or do you mean any monarch with dominion over England (Elizabeth I, Victoria, etc.)? If the former, maybe hint why it's limited to kings. If the latter, say "monarchs of England" or something like that.

but instead of speaking, it handed its sword to Bernard, and left.

Referring to him as "Mr. Scrivens" or something other than his first name would feel more clinical.

A team was dispatched to contain the site, and determine what had happened. Upon arrival, the team was attacked by the man, and, in defending themselves, accidentally terminated him.

This is a personal thing, but I'm having a hard time believing that a trained security team employed by an organization that places a premium on containment can't find a way to non-lethally incapacitate someone with a sword. Unless the sword conferred "fightan good" abilities on this guy (and if it did it probably needs to be at least hinted at in the log), the idea of a presumably healthy young man being accidentally killed in this scenario doesn't feel right to me.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but why in Scotland? Is he meant to be some kind of exile?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really feeling this one. It's okay and all, it's just isn't my cup of tea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And, in my mind, they'd list the sword as "awaiting classification pending determination of anomalous properties" rather than classifying it as an SCP before deciding whether or not it's anomalous.

when an incorporeal ghost hands a man a real sword, it's fucking anomalous.
